note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old female presents with a 5-day history of burning urination without fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge. She reports no allergies and is not pregnant. There are no systemic symptoms such as fever or flank pain to suggest upper urinary tract involvement.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings are not assessed. No laboratory or imaging data available at this time.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis)  
  - Differential: Urethritis, vaginal irritation or infection, interstitial cystitis  
  - Red flags considered: Absence of fever, flank pain, or systemic symptoms reduces concern for pyelonephritis or complicated UTI.

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage as uncomplicated UTI in urgent care setting with follow-up in primary care if symptoms worsen or persist beyond treatment course.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy appropriate for uncomplicated cystitis (e.g., nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if no allergies or contraindications). Recommend increased fluid intake and symptomatic relief with phenazopyridine if needed. Advise to complete full antibiotic course.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care if she develops fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, or if symptoms do not improve within 48–72 hours of starting antibiotics.

Patient is not pregnant.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Duration: 5 days  
- Fever: no  
- Flank pain: no  
- Discharge: no  
- Pregnancy: no
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: IDSA Guideline: Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis
